turns.name: JOHN ROBERTS
turns.text: Justice Sotomayor has the opinion of the Court in 24-275, Parrish against Honickman.

turns.name: SONIA SOTOMAYOR
turns.text: After a District Court enters its judgment in a civil case involving the government, litigants usually have 60 days to appeal. When the judgment does not reach one of the parties on time however, Congress has allowed District Courts to reopen the time for filing an appeal. In this case, a Federal District Court dismissed petitioner Donte Parrish's wrongful imprisonment suit against the government. Parrish, a federal inmate, did not receive that decision until three months after it was entered, at which point, he promptly filed a notice of appeal explaining the reason for his delay. The Fourth Circuit construed his notice as a motion to reopen the appeal period and the District Court granted reopening. After that, Parrish thought he was in the clear to proceed with his appeal. The Fourth Circuit, however, held that Parrish should have filed a second notice after the District Court granted reopening so it dismissed the appeal. This Court now holds that under the federal reopening statute and the Rules of Appellate Procedure, the first notice of appeal provided notice enough. For over a century, this Court has held that a notice of appeal filed too early can convey appellate jurisdiction so long as it makes clear who is appealing from what judgment and to what court. When a premature notice is otherwise adequate, courts treat the notice as if it had been filed on time. Nothing in the statute changed this long-standing principle and the Rules of Appellate Procedure are consistent with it. Thus, the rule remains that appeals should not be dismissed simply because they were filed too early. Here the only problem with Parrish's notice was that it was filed before the District Court granted reopening. So, with respect to the reopen period, the notice came too early. The notice was otherwise adequate so it should be treated as filed on time. Because the Fourth Circuit held otherwise, this Court now reverses the judgment below and remands the case for further proceedings. Justice Jackson filed a concurring opinion in which Justice Thomas joined. Justice Gorsuch filed a dissenting opinion.
